title: Why 'Not Enough Time' Is Rarely About Time
subtitle: A reading on what the feeling of not having enough time is actually about
excerpt: A reading on the chronic feeling of time scarcity in women's lives. Examines what the feeling is actually tracking: emotional labour, invisible work, competing role demands, and why time management advice misses the point.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. Draws on research on time use, emotional labour, and women's experience of time scarcity.

practice.whenToUse: Use when the feeling of never having enough time is chronic and time management solutions haven't helped.

content.text: A reading on the feeling of not enough time. Most people with this feeling have tried the standard solutions: lists, schedules, time-blocking, saying no. The solutions often produce marginal improvement or none. This is partly because the problem is not primarily a time-management problem. It is a different kind of problem wearing time's clothes.

Research on subjective time scarcity (the feeling of not having enough time, regardless of how many hours are available) finds that it is not strongly correlated with the actual number of hours available. People with fewer free hours do not consistently report more time scarcity than people with more. What does predict the feeling is the number of competing demands, the quality of transitions between activities, and whether the time used feels like it belongs to you.

For women specifically, the drivers include: invisible work (the mental load of coordination, anticipation, and management that doesn't appear in any time diary); emotional labour that takes cognitive and energy resources without appearing to take time; and the number of roles whose demands are simultaneous rather than sequential.
Why time management doesn't solve it

Time management addresses the scheduling problem. It does not address the invisible load, the role fragmentation, or the quality of the time available. Getting more efficient at task-execution does not reduce the mental load of the tasks that were never on the visible list. Creating time slots does not address whether the time in them is actually yours.

The result is that women who successfully implement time management techniques often still report not enough time, because they have optimised the scheduling of a problem that isn't primarily a scheduling problem.
What the question is actually asking

A more productive frame is: which demands are on me, which of them are mine to carry, and which could be redistributed? The not-enough-time feeling is often the cognitive and emotional weight of carrying too many demands, not a shortage of hours. The solution is demand reduction or redistribution, not time management.
Written for homemade.education. Draws on research on time use, subjective time scarcity, emotional labour, and women's experience of the mental load.
